topic.name: Early 20th Century Naval Warfare
topic.description: The documents discuss various naval ships and their roles during the early 20th century, particularly in the context of World War I.

Document 7: HMS Duke of Edinburgh was the lead ship of the Duke of Edinburgh-class armoured cruisers built for the Royal Navy in the mid-1900s
She participated in the Battle of Jutland in May 1916.

topic.name: Fungi and Sharks
topic.description: This cluster discusses various species of fungi and sharks, highlighting their characteristics, habitats, and ecological roles.

Document 1: Phellodon is a genus of tooth fungi in the family Bankeraceae.
Species grow in a symbiotic mycorrhizal association with trees.
Document 2: Clavaria fragilis, commonly known as fairy fingers, is a species of fungus in the family Clavariaceae.
It is widespread throughout temperate regions in the Northern Hemisphere.
Document 3: The finetooth shark (Carcharhinus isodon) is a species of requiem shark, in the family Carcharhinidae.
The diet of this species consists primarily of small bony fishes.
Document 4: Mycena maculata, commonly known as the reddish-spotted Mycena, is a species of fungus in the Mycenaceae family.
The saprobic fungus is found in Europe and North America.
Document 5: The kitefin shark or seal shark (Dalatias licha) is a species of dogfish shark in the family Dalatiidae.
This shark is fished commercially for its meat, skin, and liver oil.
Document 6: Amanita abrupta is a species of fungus in the Amanitaceae family of mushrooms.
This terrestrial species grows in mixed woods in eastern North America and eastern Asia.
Document 7: The smalltail shark (Carcharhinus porosus) is a species of requiem shark, and part of the family Carcharhinidae.
It is viviparous, meaning the developing embryos are sustained by a placental connection.

topic.name: Medieval Castles in England and Venezuela
topic.description: The documents discuss the history, construction, and significance of various medieval castles located in England and Venezuela.

Document 1: Bedford Castle was a large medieval castle in Bedford, England.
The castle played a prominent part in both the civil war of the Anarchy and the First Barons' War.
Document 2: Piel Castle is a castle situated on the south-eastern point of Piel Island, off the coast of the Furness Peninsula in north-west England.
Built in the early-14th century by John Cockerham, it was intended to oversee the trade through the local harbour.
Document 3: Santa Rosa de la Eminencia castle is a colonial castle built in the seventeenth century by the Spanish monarchy in La Asunción, Venezuela.
Its construction started on 24 March 1677 by order of governor Juan Muñoz de Gadea after a group of French pirates attacked the city.
Document 4: Brownsea Castle was originally a Device Fort constructed by Henry VIII between 1545 and 1547 to protect Poole Harbour in Dorset, England.
It was garrisoned by the local town with six soldiers and armed with eight artillery pieces.
Document 6: Skipsea Castle is a Norman motte and bailey castle near the village of Skipsea, East Riding of Yorkshire, England.
Built around 1086 by Drogo de la Beuvrière, it was designed to secure the newly conquered region.
Document 7: Dunstanburgh Castle is a 14th-century fortification on the coast of Northumberland in northern England.
The castle was built by Earl Thomas of Lancaster between 1313 and 1322.

the game has sold 3.1 million copies.

the expansion received generally favorable reviews upon its release.

topic.name: Ancient Military Conflicts
topic.description: The documents discuss various significant military engagements and wars from ancient history, focusing on conflicts involving Greek city-states and their adversaries.

Document 1: Wars of the Delian League (477 – 449 BC) were a series of campaigns fought between the Delian League of Athens and her allies, and the Achaemenid Empire of Persia.
the Delian League campaigned in Thrace and the Aegean to remove the remaining Persian garrisons from the region.
Document 2: The Battle of Long Island was the first major battle of the American Revolutionary War to take place after the United States declared its independence.
It was a victory for the British Army and the beginning of a successful campaign that gave them control of the strategically important city of New York.
Document 3: The Siege of Fort William Henry was conducted in August 1757 by French General Louis-Joseph de Montcalm against the British-held Fort William Henry.
After several days of bombardment, Monro surrendered to Montcalm, whose force included nearly 2,000 Indians from a large number of tribes.
Document 4: The Battle of Solachon was fought in 586 CE in northern Mesopotamia between the East Roman (Byzantine) forces and the Sassanid Persians.
The engagement was part of the long and inconclusive Byzantine–Sassanid War of 572–591.
Document 5: The Battle of Tellicherry was a naval action fought off the Indian port of Tellicherry between British and French warships on 18 November 1791 during the Third Anglo-Mysore War.
Although under normal circumstances the battle might have provoked a diplomatic incident, the upheavals of the ongoing French Revolution meant that the despatches had little effect.
Document 6: The Battle of the Eurymedon was a double battle, taking place both on water and land, between the Delian League of Athens and her Allies, and the Persian Empire of Xerxes I.
It forms part of the Wars of the Delian League, itself part of the larger Greco-Persian Wars.
Document 7: The first Persian invasion of Greece, during the Persian Wars, began in 492 BC, and ended with the decisive Athenian victory at the Battle of Marathon in 490 BC.
These cities had supported the cities of Ionia during their revolt against Persian rule, thus incurring the wrath of Darius.

Document 6: The 42nd Military Police Brigade is a military police brigade of the United States Army based at Joint Base Lewis-McChord, Washington.

Document 7: The 31st / 51st Battalion was an infantry battalion of the Australian Army, which served during World War II.
The battalion undertook garrison duties in Dutch New Guinea in 1943 – 44.

topic.name: Railway Systems and Infrastructure
topic.description: The documents discuss various aspects of railway systems, including rolling stock, station history, and rail links in different regions.

Document 1: The rolling stock of Oslo Metro, Norway has consisted of three classes: T1000 / T1300, T2000 and MX3000.
All trains feature automatic train protection and step-free access from the platforms.
Document 2: East Finchley is a London Underground station in East Finchley in the London Borough of Barnet, north London.
The station was opened in 1867 as part of the Great Northern Railway's line between Finsbury Park and Edgware stations.
Document 3: Flytoget, the Airport Express Train is a Norwegian high-speed airport rail link connecting Oslo Airport, Gardermoen to Oslo Central Station.

Document 4: Grand Central Station was a passenger railroad terminal in downtown Chicago, Illinois, from 1890 to 1969.

Document 5: Nailsea and Backwell railway station, on the Bristol to Exeter Line, is in the village of Backwell.

Document 6: The Flekkefjord Line is a 17.1-kilometre abandoned branch line to the Sørland Line.

Document 7: The Bømlafjord Tunnel is a subsea road tunnel under Bømlafjorden which connects the island of Føyno to the mainland.

The Bill of Rights was originally proposed as a measure to assuage Anti-Federalist opposition to Constitutional ratification.

topic.name: Transportation and Vehicles
topic.description: The common topic revolves around various modes of transportation including ambulances, military aircraft, steam locomotives, and heavy vehicles.

Document 1: An ambulance is a vehicle for transportation of sick or injured people

the term also includes a wide range of urgent and non-urgent vehicles including trucks, vans, bicycles, motorbikes, station wagons, buses, helicopters, fixed-wing aircraft, boats, and even hospital ships.
Document 2: The Dornier Do 17 was a World War II German light bomber
designed as a Schnellbomber, a light bomber which, in theory, would be so fast that it could outrun defending fighter aircraft.
Document 3: The SR Q1 class is a type of austerity steam locomotive constructed during the Second World War
designed by Oliver Bulleid for use on the intensive freight turns experienced during wartime.
Document 4: The works had its own internal standard gauge railway for over 100 years
which used three steam and three diesel locomotives over the course of its existence.
Document 5: The North Staffordshire Railway was a British railway company
formed in 1845 to promote a number of lines in the Staffordshire Potteries.
Document 6: The BR Standard Class 7, otherwise known as the Britannia Class, is a class of 4-6-2 Pacific steam locomotive
designed by Robert Riddles for use by British Railways for mixed traffic duties.
Document 7: Vanajan Autotehdas Oy was a producer of heavy vehicles based in Hämeenlinna, Finland
the company started producing bus chassis in 1950.

Tightrope walkers, hot-air balloon ascents, concerts and fireworks provided entertainment.
Document 4: painting by James Abbott McNeill Whistler
Whistler intended to emphasise his artistic philosophy of corresponding arts.

came to represent his 2008 presidential campaign.

markedly influential on other film-makers and ultimately on the development of narrative film.

topic.name: Notable Figures and Organizations
topic.description: This cluster discusses significant individuals and organizations in various fields such as education, film, technology, politics, and management.

1: Curley Byrd was an American university administrator, educator, athlete, coach, and politician.
Byrd began a long association with the University of Maryland as an undergraduate in 1905.
2: Salman Aristo is an Indonesian screenwriter and film director best known for his work on Ayat-Ayat Cinta, Laskar Pelangi, Garuda di Dadaku, and Sang Penari.
Aristo became interested in films from a young age, though he did not consider a career in the industry until after graduating from university.
3: Hightail is a cloud service that lets users send, receive, digitally sign and synchronize files.
The company was renamed Hightail in 2013.
4: Robert Latham Owen was one of the first two U.S. senators from Oklahoma.
He served in the Senate between 1907 and 1925.
5: Magic Alex, born Yanni Alexis Mardas, was involved with The Beatles and was head of Apple Electronics.
Mardas impressed John Lennon with the Nothing Box.
6: McKinsey & Company is a worldwide management consulting firm.
It conducts qualitative and quantitative analysis in order to evaluate management decisions across the public and private sectors.
7: Bridgewater Associates is an American investment management firm founded by Ray Dalio in 1975.
The firm serves institutional clients including pension funds, endowments, foundations, foreign governments and central banks.

topic.name: Arguments for God's Existence
topic.description: The documents discuss various philosophical arguments that seek to establish the existence of God through morality, design, and the nature of conscience.

Document 1: The argument from morality is an argument for the existence of God.
Arguments from moral normativity observe some aspect of morality and argue that God is the best or only explanation for this.
Document 2: The teleological argument is an argument for the existence of God based on perceived evidence of deliberate design in the natural or physical world.
It is historically closely associated with the concept of Natural Theology.
Document 3: Religious views of conscience usually see it as linked to a morality inherent in all humans, to a beneficent universe and/or to divinity.
The extent to which conscience informs moral judgment before an action and whether such moral judgments are or should be based in reason has occasioned debate.

topic.name: Texas History and Geography
topic.description: The documents explore historical and geographical aspects of various locations in Texas, highlighting their significance and development over time.

1: The history of Galveston, Texas, begins with the archaeological record of Native Americans who used the island.
Galveston was founded in 1836 by Matthew Sabo and served as the capital of the Republic of Texas.
2: St. Charles Bay is an inlet of Aransas Bay in Aransas County, Texas.
The nomadic Karankawa Indians used the bay for hunting before the arrival of Europeans.
3: Lavaca Bay is a northwestern extension of the Matagorda Bay system found mostly in Calhoun County, Texas.
The ports of Port Lavaca and Point Comfort have been established on the bay, and are the main areas of human habitation.
7: The Delta County Courthouse is a historic, three-story courthouse building in the city of Cooper, in Delta County, Texas.
Delta County was established on July 29, 1870, by the Twelfth Legislature of Texas.

topic.name: Religious Architecture and History
topic.description: The documents explore various religious figures, places of worship, and their historical significance across different Christian denominations.

1: Peter of Bruys was a popular French religious teacher, criticized infant baptism, and was killed by an angry mob.
He began his preaching in Dauphiné and Provence probably between 1117 and 1120.
2: Akhtala is a 10th-century fortified Armenian Apostolic Church monastery.
The main church at the compound is famous for its highly artistic frescoes.
3: The Church of the Holy Mother of God is a medieval Eastern Orthodox church in Serbia.
The church is notable for its unusual architectural style and richly decorated interior.
4: Anshei Israel Synagogue is a historic synagogue located in Lisbon, Connecticut, built in 1936.
The synagogue reflects the early Jewish community's need for public worship.
5: The Catholic Church is the largest Christian church, with more than 1.27 billion members.
It has played a prominent role in the history of Western civilization.
7: Bath Abbey is a former Benedictine monastery and one of the largest examples of Perpendicular Gothic architecture.
It is an active place of worship and a heritage museum.

topic.name: Entertainment and Comedy Events
topic.description: The documents discuss various entertainment events, particularly focusing on comedy tours, television shows, and notable figures in the comedy and poker scenes.

Document 1: comedy tour by American comedian and talk show host Conan O'Brien
O'Brien performed 42 shows in the United States and Canada.
Document 3: mock rivalry that occurred among late night talk show hosts Stephen Colbert, Conan O'Brien and Jon Stewart
the feud was concocted by the three comedians as a result of the 2007 – 2008 Writers Guild of America strike.
Document 4: concert tour by Bruce Springsteen and the E Street Band
the tour placed an emphasis on performing at music festivals.
Document 5: Michael Matusow is an American professional poker player
a four-time World Series of Poker bracelet winner.

topic.name: Cultural and Linguistic Heritage
topic.description: The documents explore various aspects of cultural and linguistic heritage, including etymology, traditional practices, and endangered languages.

Document 1: The word orange is derived from a Dravidian language, and it passed through numerous other languages including Sanskrit and Old French before reaching the English language.
The earliest uses of the word in English refer to the fruit.
Document 2: Marchantiophyta are a division of non-vascular land plants commonly referred to as hepatics or liverworts.
They have a gametophyte-dominant life cycle.
Document 3: Cantiones sacrae is a collection of forty different pieces of vocal sacred music on Latin texts.
The general title Cantiones sacrae was common at the time and was used by many composers.
Document 4: Tefillin are a set of small black leather boxes containing scrolls of parchment inscribed with verses from the Torah.
The Torah commands that they should be worn to serve as a 'sign' and 'remembrance'.
Document 5: The Naulakha Pavilion is a prominent white marble personal chamber located beside the Sheesh Mahal courtyard.
It became inscribed as a UNESCO World Heritage Site.
Document 6: Johannes Rebmann was a German missionary and explorer credited with feats including being the first European to find Kilimanjaro.
His work there is also thought to have had effects on future African expeditions by Europeans.
Document 7: Wagiman is a near-extinct indigenous Australian language spoken by fewer than 10 people.
Wagiman is notable within linguistics for its complex system of verbal morphology.

topic.name: Historical Sites and Figures
topic.description: The documents discuss significant historical sites, figures, and events, highlighting their cultural and architectural importance.

1: Dacre Castle is a moated tower house in the village of Dacre
2: Rohm and Haas Corporate Headquarters in Philadelphia
completed in 1964, the building was the first private investment for the urban renewal
3: Molly Brant was a Mohawk woman who was influential in the era of the American Revolution
she fled to British Canada, where she worked as an intermediary between British officials and the Iroquois
4: HMS Diana was a D-class destroyer of the Royal Navy
5: The Ipswich serial murders took place between 30 October and 10 December 2006
the bodies of five murdered women were discovered at different locations near Ipswich
6: William Ford Robinson Stanley was a British inventor with 78 patents
he designed and made precision drawing and mathematical instruments
7: Dubh Artach is a remote skerry of basalt rock off the west coast of Scotland
A lighthouse designed by Thomas Stevenson was erected between 1867 and 1872

topic.name: Cultural Impact of Music and Arts
topic.description: The documents explore various aspects of music and arts, highlighting their influence on culture, public perception, and societal issues.

Document 1: Re-Invention World Tour was the sixth concert tour by American singer-songwriter Madonna.
The tour garnered positive reception from contemporary critics.
Document 3: Austria participated in the Eurovision Song Contest 2014 with the song 'Rise Like a Phoenix'.
This was Austria's second win in the contest, having previously won in 1966.
Document 4: 'Freedom' is a song by English girl group Sugababes.
Despite the controversy surrounding its release, the song received positive reviews from critics.
Document 6: 'Stop' is a song by the British pop group Spice Girls.
The song received mostly positive reviews from music critics, with many of them complimenting the Motown influences and production.

topic.name: Scientology Controversies
topic.description: The documents discuss various controversies surrounding the Church of Scientology, including protests against its practices and attempts to manipulate information online.

Document 1: Project Chanology was a protest movement against the practices of the Church of Scientology by members of Anonymous.
The project was started in response to the Church of Scientology's attempts to remove material from a highly publicized interview.
Document 2: The Church of Scientology has long had a controversial history on the Internet.
Disputes began in 2005, with users disagreeing about whether or not to describe Scientology as an abusive cult or religion.

topic.name: Cultural References in Media
topic.description: The documents discuss various cultural references and figures in music, television, and folklore, highlighting their significance and impact within popular culture.

The duo's independent record label had been renamed KLF Communications.

It is often thought of as an ancient construction, though the earliest mention of it dates to the late 17th century.
Document 3: Deep Throat serves as an informant, leaking information to FBI Special Agent Fox Mulder.
The character was inspired by the historical Deep Throat, Mark Felt.

He was involved in two failed conspiracies to open the Spanish-controlled Mississippi River to American traffic.
Document 5: Montag came to prominence after being cast in the MTV reality television series The Hills.

topic.name: Major League Baseball History
topic.description: Documents discussing significant events, players, and milestones in Major League Baseball (MLB).

1: Zack Greinke is an American professional baseball pitcher for the Arizona Diamondbacks of Major League Baseball (MLB).
He previously played for the Kansas City Royals, Milwaukee Brewers, Los Angeles Angels of Anaheim, and Los Angeles Dodgers.
3: The 1962 National League tie-breaker series was a three-game playoff series at the conclusion of Major League Baseball's (MLB) 1962 regular season.
The playoff series was necessary after both teams finished the season with identical win-loss records of 101-61.
4: Oscar Francisco Taveras was a Dominican-Canadian professional baseball outfielder who played one season for the St. Louis Cardinals of Major League Baseball (MLB).
He was a consensus top-five minor league prospect in 2013 and 2014.
